subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 1! In this unit you will become confident with numbers of every size – from large whole numbers in the millions all the way down to tiny decimals in the thousandths. You will read them, write them, compare them, take them apart, scale them up and down, and round them. These are the numbers that run the real world. When Hassan checks that the water tanker holds 12,500 litres, when a scientist measures a butterfly wingspan of 0.375 inches, or when Amina counts out shillings at the Hargeisa market, they are all working with place value.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Read, write and understand whole numbers up to the millions, and decimals with up to three decimal places (tenths, hundredths, thousandths).
Explain the value of each digit in a number using its place.
Compose numbers by combining place value parts, and decompose numbers into their parts.
Regroup a decimal in different ways while keeping the same value.
Compare and order decimal numbers using place value.
Multiply and divide whole numbers and decimals by 10, 100 and 1000, explaining how the digits move.
Round decimals to the nearest whole number, tenth, hundredth or thousandth.
Read and order negative numbers on a number line and in real contexts such as temperature.
Use estimation to check that answers are sensible, and apply these skills to money, measurement and science problems.

concepts.title: Place Value with Whole Numbers and Decimals
concepts.explanation: Place value tells us what each digit in a number represents, based on its position. You already know this for whole numbers. In 352,714 the 3 means three hundred thousand, the 5 means fifty thousand, and so on. Each place to the left is ten times larger than the place to its right. Reading from the right, the whole-number places are ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, ten thousands, hundred thousands and millions. Decimals continue this same pattern in the other direction. When we move right past the decimal point,…
concepts.example: The 1 is in the tens place, worth 10.

concepts.title: Reading and Writing Decimal Numbers
concepts.explanation: There are two good ways to read a decimal aloud, and you should be comfortable with both. The first way simply reads the digits after the point one at a time. So 13.136 is read as “thirteen point one three six.” Notice we do not say “thirteen point one hundred and thirty-six” – after the point, we name each digit separately. The second way uses the name of the last place. Because the smallest digit in 13.136 sits in the thousandths place, we can read it as “thirteen and one hundred thirty-six thousandths.” The…
concepts.example: Writing a decimal from words works in reverse. Take “seven and twenty-four thousandths.” The word and marks the decimal point, so the whole part is 7. “Twenty-four thousandths” means 24 in the last three decimal places,…

concepts.title: Composing and Decomposing Numbers
concepts.explanation: Composing means putting parts together to make a whole number. Decomposing means breaking a number into its parts. These are two sides of the same skill, and they prove that you truly understand what a number is made of. The most natural way to decompose is by place value. For example, 2.139 breaks apart as 2 + 0.1 + 0.03 + 0.009, which we can say aloud as “2 ones + 1 tenth + 3 hundredths + 9 thousandths.” This is called expanded form. Try it with 8.146: the parts are 8 ones, 1 tenth, 4 hundredths and 6…
concepts.example: But a number can be split in many different ways, not just by single place values. This is called regrouping, and it keeps the same value while showing it differently. Look at 0.546. We can write it as 0.5 + 0.04 +…

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering Decimals
concepts.explanation: To compare two decimals, work from left to right, one place at a time, and stop as soon as the digits differ. Start with the whole-number part; if those are equal, move to the tenths, then the hundredths, then the thousandths. Compare 0.67 and 0.607. The whole parts are both 0. The tenths are both 6. Now the hundredths: 0.67 has 7 hundredths while 0.607 has 0 hundredths. Since 7 is more than 0, 0.67 > 0.607. A powerful trick is to give the numbers the same number of decimal places by adding zeros: 0.67 becomes…
concepts.example: This is why the biggest myth in decimals – “more digits means a bigger number” – is false. The number 0.607 has more digits than 0.67, yet it is smaller. Length does not decide size; place value does. Always line up the…

concepts.title: Multiplying and Dividing by 10, 100 and 1000
concepts.explanation: When we multiply or divide by 10, 100 or 1000, the digits move to new places while keeping the same value – it only looks as though the decimal point has moved. Getting this picture right is the key to the whole idea. When you multiply by a power of ten, the number gets larger, so the digits move to the LEFT:

concepts.title: Rounding Decimal Numbers
concepts.explanation: Rounding replaces a number with a nearby, simpler number that is easier to say and work with. It is easier to report that “Usain Bolt ran 100 metres in under 10 seconds” than to quote his exact time of 9.63 seconds. We round all the time – for money, measurements and estimates. The method is always the same. First, decide which place you are rounding to. Then look at the very next digit to its right. If that next digit is 0, 1, 2, 3 or 4, you round down (leave the rounding digit unchanged). If it is 5, 6, 7, 8 or…
concepts.example: Rounding to the nearest tenth – look at the hundredths digit:

methods.title: How to compare two decimals
methods.example: What is the value of the digit 5 in the number 12.357?
steps: Give both numbers the same number of decimal places by adding zeros.
Compare the whole-number parts first.
If equal, compare the tenths, then hundredths, then thousandths.
Write the correct symbol: >, < or =.

methods.title: How to multiply or divide by a power of ten
methods.example: Write “seven and twenty-four thousandths” in figures.
steps: Count the zeros in 10, 100 or 1000 – that is how many places to move.
Multiplying moves the digits left; dividing moves them right.
Fill any empty places with zeros as placeholders.

methods.title: How to round a decimal
steps: Decide which place you are rounding to.
Look at the single digit immediately to its right.
Round up if it is 5 or more, down if it is 4 or less.
Drop everything after the rounding place (and carry if a 9 rounds up).

methods.title: Ordering decimals
steps: Solution: Rewrite each with three decimal places: 0.670, 0.607, 0.700, 0.067.
Compare place by place.
The order from smallest is 0.067 < 0.607 < 0.67 < 0.7.
Adding the zeros made the comparison easy.

methods.title: A chain of powers of ten
methods.example: Follow the number: start with 0.6, then ×10, then ×100, then ÷1000.
steps: So the chain is 0.6 → 6 → 600 → 0.6.
Interestingly, it returns to where it began, because ×10 ×100 = ×1000, which the final ÷1000 undoes.

methods.title: Rounding to the nearest hundredth
steps: Solution: The hundredths digit is 4.
Look at the next digit to the right, the thousandths digit, which is 7.
Since 7 is 5 or more, round the hundredths up from 4 to 5.
The answer is 15.85.

workedExamples.title: The value of a digit
workedExamples.prompt: What is the value of the digit 5 in the number 12.357?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Read the places after the point: 3 is tenths, 5 is hundredths, 7 is thousandths. So the 5 sits in the hundredths place and is worth 0.05 (five hundredths). A common wrong answer is “thousandths,” but the 7 – not the 5 – is in the thousandths place.

workedExamples.title: Words into a number
workedExamples.prompt: Write “seven and twenty-four thousandths” in figures.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The word “and” marks the decimal point, so the whole part is 7. “Twenty-four thousandths” must end in the thousandths column: 24 written across the last three places is 0-2-4. So the answer is 7.024. The zero in the tenths place is essential.

workedExamples.title: Expanded form
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 8 ones, 1 tenth, 4 hundredths, 6 thousandths, giving 8 + 0.1 + 0.04 + 0.006. Check by adding the parts: 8 + 0.1 + 0.04 + 0.006 = 8.146. It matches.

workedExamples.title: Ordering decimals
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Rewrite each with three decimal places: 0.670, 0.607, 0.700, 0.067. Compare place by place. The order from smallest is 0.067 < 0.607 < 0.67 < 0.7. Adding the zeros made the comparison easy.

workedExamples.title: A chain of powers of ten
workedExamples.prompt: Follow the number: start with 0.6, then ×10, then ×100, then ÷1000.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 0.6 × 10 = 6; 6 × 100 = 600; 600 ÷ 1000 = 0.6. So the chain is 0.6 → 6 → 600 → 0.6. Interestingly, it returns to where it began, because ×10 ×100 = ×1000, which the final ÷1000 undoes.

workedExamples.title: Rounding to the nearest hundredth
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The hundredths digit is 4. Look at the next digit to the right, the thousandths digit, which is 7. Since 7 is 5 or more, round the hundredths up from 4 to 5. The answer is 15.85.

workedExamples.title: A scaling word problem
workedExamples.prompt: A recipe needs 3.75 kg of flour for 10 people. How much is needed for 100 people?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 100 people is 10 times as many as 10 people, so we need 10 times as much flour: 3.75 × 10 = 37.5 kg. The digits moved one place to the left.

workedExamples.title: Comparing temperatures
workedExamples.prompt: One morning a fridge reads −3°C and a freezer reads −18°C. Which is colder, and by how much?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: On the number line −18 is further to the left than −3, so the freezer is colder. The difference is the number of steps between them: from −18 up to −3 is 15 steps, so the freezer is 15 degrees colder.

workedExamples.title: Round 8.2749 to
workedExamples.prompt: Round 8.2749 to: a) nearest thousandth b) nearest hundredth c) nearest tenth d) nearest whole number.

workedExamples.title: Express 0.045 metres in
workedExamples.prompt: Express 0.045 metres in a) centimetres (×100) b) millimetres (×1000).

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Put these temperatures in order from coldest to warmest: 4°C, −2°C, −7°C, 0°C, 3°C.

activities.title: Decimal Detective
steps: Be a Decimal Detective and decode mysterious numbers. For each number, write the value of every digit.
Take the number 4.237. Write: the 4 is ___ ones, the 2 is ___ tenths, the 3 is ___ hundredths, the 7 is ___ thousandths.
Now say the whole number in words two different ways.
Do the same detective report for 15.068 and 0.405.
Challenge: make a decimal where the thousandths digit is larger than the tenths digit, and decode it.

activities.title: Build a Place Value Grid with Coins
activities.materials: small objects to stand for ones, tenths, hundredths and thousandths (for example large stones, bottle caps, beans and seeds).
steps: Draw a grid with columns: Ones, point, Tenths, Hundredths, Thousandths.
Ask a family member to call out a decimal such as 3.456.
Place the right number of objects in each column to build it.
Say the number aloud, then decompose it: 3 + 0.4 + 0.05 + 0.006.

activities.title: The Power of Ten Race Track
steps: Draw a race track with checkpoints. A starting number races round, and at each checkpoint it is multiplied or divided by a power of ten.
Racer 1 starts at 3.45: ×10, then ×100, then ÷10. Write the number after each checkpoint.
Racer 2 starts at 67: ×1000, then ÷100, then ×10.
Racer 3 starts at 0.8: ×100, then ×10, then ÷1000.
Design your own race for 2.5 with four checkpoints and give it to a friend to solve.

activities.title: Rounding Restaurant
steps: You own a restaurant, and every price must be rounded for different purposes – some to the nearest whole shilling, some to the nearest ten cents.
Butterfly Burger costs 8.67. Round it to the nearest whole and to the nearest tenth.
Place Value Pasta costs 15.89. Round it both ways.
The freezer reads −18.7°C. Round it to the nearest whole degree.
Invent three more menu prices and round each one two ways.

activities.title: Real-World Number Hunt (Family Task)
activities.materials: your home, and any prices, labels or measurements you can find.
steps: Hunt for ten decimal numbers around the house – on packets, rulers, scales or bills.
For each one, write where you found it and what each digit is worth.
Choose five and explain when someone might round them and why.
Pick one measurement and show what happens when you multiply it by 10 and by 100.

activities.title: Number Line Temperatures (Investigation)
steps: Draw a number line from −10 to 10 with zero in the middle.
Mark these temperatures on it: −7, −2, 0, 3, 8.
Over one day, record the temperature in the early morning and at midday, and mark both.
Work out how many degrees the temperature rose between your two readings.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand decimals best? Which concept – place value, powers of ten, rounding or negatives – do you feel most confident about now? What was the trickiest part, and what would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Place Value Rule
rules.text: Each place to the left is ten times bigger; each place to the right is ten times smaller. Places after the point: tenths (0.1), hundredths (0.01), thousandths (0.001). In 13.136: 1 ten, 3 ones, 1 tenth, 3 hundredths, 6 thousandths.

rules.title: Powers of Ten Rule
rules.text: Multiply by 10, 100, 1000 = digits move Left = number gets Larger. Divide by 10, 100, 1000 = digits move Right = number gets smaller. The number of zeros tells you how many places to move.

rules.title: Rounding Rule
rules.text: Look at the one digit just to the right of the place you are rounding to. 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 round down; 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 round up. Nearest tenth: look at hundredths. Nearest whole: look at tenths.

rules.title: Negative Numbers Rule
rules.text: Numbers left of zero are negative and get smaller as you move further left. Negative 5 is smaller and colder than negative 2.

terms: What a digit is worth from its position
Separates the whole part from the parts
The first, second and third decimal places
Join parts into a number / break a number into parts
Replace a number with a nearby simpler one
commonMistakes: 0.3 is 3 tenths; 0.03 is 3 hundredths – ten times smaller
0.607 < 0.67; add zeros and compare place by place
Multiply = left = larger; divide = right = smaller
Look only at the next digit to the right

realProblems.prompt: A baker uses 3.75 kg of flour for 10 loaves. How much for a) 100 loaves b) 1000 loaves c) 1 loaf?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: The distance from Nairobi to a village is 234.56 km. A GPS rounds to the nearest kilometre. What does it show?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A scientist measures 0.0456 kg. Express it in grams, then round to the nearest gram.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A city’s population is 234,567. Round it a) to the nearest thousand b) to the nearest ten thousand.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: At Fajr the temperature is −5°C and by Asr it is 8°C. By how many degrees has it risen?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Fabric costs 460 shillings per metre. Find the cost of a) 2.5 m b) 0.75 m c) 10 m.
realProblems.answer: a) 2.5 × 460 = 1150 shillings, b) 0.75 × 460 = 345 shillings, c) 10 × 460 = 4600 shillings.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: a) 2.5 × 460 = 1150 shillings, b) 0.75 × 460 = 345 shillings, c) 10 × 460 = 4600 shillings.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A number multiplied by 100 gives 456.7. What is the original number?
A number rounds to 12 to the nearest whole number and rounds to 11.8 to the nearest tenth. Give one number it could be.
The factory records masses 2.345, 2.354, 2.435, 2.453, 2.534, 2.543 kg. Rounded to the nearest hundredth, how many different values are there?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The rounded values are 2.35, 2.35, 2.44, 2.45, 2.53, 2.54, giving 5 different values.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find a decimal with three decimal places that, multiplied by 1000, gives a whole number, rounds to 7.3 to the nearest tenth, and whose digits add to 15.
Explain the key idea in Rounding Decimal Numbers.

It is easier to report that “Usain Bolt ran 100 metres in under 10 seconds” than to quote his exact time of 9.63 seconds. We round all the time – for money, measurements and estimates. The method is always the same. First, decide which place you are rounding to. Then look at the very next digit to its right. If that next digit is 0, 1, 2, 3 or 4, you round down (leave the rounding digit unchanged). If it is 5, 6, 7, 8 or…

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 9 is in the ones place, so it is worth 9.

questions.question: What does “Digit” mean?
options: What a digit is worth from its position
Separates the whole part from the parts
The first, second and third decimal places
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Digit means A single symbol from 0 to 9.

options: What a digit is worth from its position

The first, second and third decimal places
Join parts into a number / break a number into parts

selfAssessment: I can read, write and understand whole numbers up to the millions, and decimals with up to three decimal places (tenths, hundredths, thousandths).
I can explain the value of each digit in a number using its place.
I can compose numbers by combining place value parts, and decompose numbers into their parts.
I can regroup a decimal in different ways while keeping the same value.
I can compare and order decimal numbers using place value.
I can multiply and divide whole numbers and decimals by 10, 100 and 1000, explaining how the digits move.
I can round decimals to the nearest whole number, tenth, hundredth or thousandth.
I can read and order negative numbers on a number line and in real contexts such as temperature.
